summary: Removed disabled and apparently unused GitHub Actions/container infrastructure for Windows build smoke tests and assembly dump publishing. The PR deleted the dump-asm workflow and its package scripts, deleted the build-windows container package scripts, removed the disabled build-windows job from build-melee.yml, removed Windows package publishing logic from publish-packages.yml, and renamed the remaining Linux make build job to a more generic Make job. Evidence is limited to the diff because the PR had no body, review comments, or issue comments.
what_changed: Deleted .github/workflows/dump-asm.yml, which had defined a disabled dump-asm job plus a push-dump job for updating a dump branch with generated assembly, map, README, and ctx.c artifacts.

Deleted the .github/packages/dump-asm container entrypoint and README generator scripts. The removed entrypoint cloned /input, symlinked /opt/mwcc_compiler, ran make with GENERATE_MAP=1, copied GALE01.map, generated README.md, ran tools/parse_map.py, and generated ctx.c via tools/m2ctx/m2ctx.py.

Deleted the .github/packages/build-windows Dockerfile and PowerShell setup/entrypoint scripts. These had installed Windows tooling with scoop, set up a Python venv, downloaded mwcc compilers, and ran make through MinGW bash in a Windows Server Core container.
Removed the disabled build-windows job from .github/workflows/build-melee.yml. That job had targeted windows-latest and tested GENERATE_MAP=1 and NON_MATCHING=1 through the build-windows container.
Renamed the remaining make-based Linux job in build-melee.yml from build-linux/Linux to build-make/Make.
Moved the check-issues job lower in build-melee.yml after build-ninja; its contents appear preserved.
In publish-packages.yml, removed the dump-asm test step and removed the entire disabled build-windows package publishing job.
In publish-packages.yml, added if: false to the remaining build-linux package publishing job, disabling that package publication path as shown in the diff.
smart_moves: Deleted whole obsolete package directories and workflows rather than leaving disabled jobs with if: false scattered through CI.
Kept the issue-checking job by relocating it instead of accidentally removing it during the cleanup.
Renamed the active make build job to Make, avoiding a misleading Linux label once Windows-specific CI was removed.

Removed CI paths that had substantial maintenance complexity: Windows Server Core images, PowerShell native-command exit-code handling, scoop setup, compiler download caching, dump artifact publishing, and branch-push automation.
The diff removes slow or disabled infrastructure that the old Dockerfile itself described as only a Windows developer-experience smoke test and 'excruciatingly slow'.
decomp_lessons: Disabled CI jobs and package definitions still impose maintenance cost; if a workflow is intentionally no longer used, deleting it can make the active build matrix easier to reason about.
Assembly/context dumps generated from CI are useful only if actively maintained. Otherwise, stale dump pipelines can obscure the current source of truth for maps, asm, and m2ctx output.

Windows decomp build support had nontrivial shell and tooling differences: PowerShell 5.1 does not reliably fail on native command errors, the Makefile did not support cmd or PowerShell directly, and the removed workflow used MinGW bash to run make.

Containerized CI for this project relied on project-specific compiler placement conventions such as tools/mwcc_compiler and generated outputs under build/ssbm.us.1.2; future replacements should preserve or explicitly update those assumptions.

The old dump-asm pipeline documented a reproducible artifact set for decomp work: GALE01.map, README.md with calcprogress output, parsed map data, and ctx.c from m2ctx. If a dump workflow is rebuilt, these are the evidence-backed artifacts to consider.
naming_conventions: Workflow job names used concise display labels such as Make, Ninja, and Issues.
Container package targets followed .github/packages/<target>/Dockerfile with target names such as build-windows and dump-asm.
Published image suffixes were derived from matrix target names like build-windows and dump-asm.
Build flags in CI were passed through MAKE_FLAGS with values such as GENERATE_MAP=1 and NON_MATCHING=1.
Generated map artifacts used versioned build paths such as build/${{ matrix.version }}/**/*.MAP and, in the deleted dump script, build/ssbm.us.1.2/GALE01.map.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Removed dump-asm automation that built with GENERATE_MAP=1 and MAX_ERRORS=1, then copied GALE01.map and generated ctx.c for decomp.me using tools/m2ctx/m2ctx.py -pn.
Removed README generation that ran tools/calcprogress/calcprogress.py with --dol build/ssbm.us.1.2/main.dol, --map build/ssbm.us.1.2/GALE01.map, --asm-obj-ext .s.o, and --old-map true.
Removed a Windows smoke-test path that ran make via MinGW bash because the Makefile did not support cmd or PowerShell directly.
No C/ASM matching code, symbol names, structs, headers, or source decompilation logic changed in this PR.
review_feedback: No review comments, reviews, issue comments, or PR body text were present in the provided context.
Because there was no human discussion excerpt, reviewer rationale for removal cannot be confirmed beyond the title and diff.
follow_up_queries: When did the build-windows and dump-asm jobs become disabled with if: false?
Was dump branch artifact generation replaced by another workflow or external process?
Why was publish-packages.yml build-linux set to if: false in the same cleanup?
Are Windows developer build instructions still tested anywhere after removing build-windows?
Is there a current documented way to generate GALE01.map and ctx.c for decomp.me after dump-asm removal?
